# ArkTS Static Types and Application Migration Guide

[TOC]

## 📌 1. Prerequisites

HarmonyOS application development faces dual demands for performance optimization and architecture evolution. As the official language for HarmonyOS native application development, ArkTS extends and enhances the TypeScript ecosystem in a targeted manner to meet core requirements.

### 1.1 Document Purpose

#### 1.1.1 Why ArkTS Static Types Are Needed

ArkTS static types address the following core requirements:

**Improving Application Performance**

ArkTS static types significantly enhance application performance through strict type constraints and low runtime overhead design:

- **Low Runtime Overhead**: By adding static type features, runtime type checking and optimization overhead are reduced, achieving higher application performance
- **Compile-time Optimization**: The static type system enables the compiler to perform deep optimization during the compilation phase, improving code execution efficiency
- **Efficient Concurrency Support**: Supports a multi-threaded concurrency model with shared memory, avoiding serialization overhead and achieving true high-performance parallel computing
- **AOT Compilation Optimization**: Supports AOT pre-compilation technology to pre-compile hot code into machine code, further improving execution efficiency

**Enhancing Code Robustness**

The core advantage of the static type system is the ability to detect potential issues during the compilation phase:

- **Type Safety**: Compile-time enforced type checking avoids runtime type errors and significantly reduces application crash rates
- **Null Safety**: Effectively prevents null pointer exceptions through strict null value checking mechanisms
- **API Contracts**: Clear type definitions serve as API contracts, making interface usage more standardized and secure

**Supporting Efficient Concurrent Programming**

Modern applications need to fully utilize multi-core CPU capabilities. ArkTS static types provide powerful support in concurrent programming:

- **Shared Memory Concurrency Model**: Supports memory sharing between threads, avoiding serialization overhead and achieving high-performance concurrency across all scenarios
- **Type-safe Concurrency APIs**: Provides type-safe concurrent programming interfaces, making multi-threaded development simpler and more reliable

#### 1.1.2 Document Positioning

This document aims to provide comprehensive guidance for application developers migrating from ArkTS dynamic types to ArkTS static types, including:

- **Knowledge System**: Systematically introduces ArkTS static type language features, runtime mechanisms, development tools, etc.
- **Migration Methods**: Provides phased, actionable migration processes and methodologies
- **Real-world Cases**: Demonstrates key decisions and best practices during the migration process through real-world scenario cases
- **Problem Diagnosis**: Helps developers quickly locate and resolve common issues during the migration process

### 1.2 Target Audience

This document is suitable for the following developer groups:

**Application developers interested in ArkTS static types who need to perform parallelization refactoring**

- Have experience with HarmonyOS application development
- Want to understand the new features and advantages of ArkTS static types
- Existing applications have performance bottlenecks and hope to improve performance through parallelization refactoring
- Applications need to handle a large number of compute-intensive tasks and need to fully utilize multi-core CPU capabilities
- Applications have high requirements for response speed and hope to optimize startup time and runtime efficiency through static types

#### 1.2.1 Prerequisite Knowledge

To better understand the content of this document, readers are recommended to have the following knowledge base:

- **TypeScript Basics**: Familiar with TypeScript or JavaScript syntax and type systems
- **HarmonyOS Application Development Experience**: At least 6 months of HarmonyOS application development experience
- **Concurrent Programming Concepts**: Understanding of basic concepts such as threads, processes, locks, race conditions, etc.
- **HarmonyOS Application Architecture**: Understanding of basic concepts such as Ability, UI frameworks, data management, etc.

#### 1.2.2 First-time Reading Recommendations

If you are encountering ArkTS static types for the first time, it is recommended to read in the following order:

1. **[Prerequisites](#1-prerequisites)** → Understand background and terminology
2. **[ArkTS Static Types Overview](A2-arkts-static-types-overview)** → Establish overall understanding and identify scenarios suitable for refactoring
3. **[Migrating Applications to ArkTS Static Types](#3-migrating-applications-to-arkts-static-types)** → Understand the complete migration methodology, including scenario identification, module refactoring, and performance acceptance
4. **[ArkTS Static Type Migration Cases](#4-arkts-static-type-migration-cases)** → Refer to real-world cases and learn (single codebase supporting dynamic/static types, ArkUI parallelization refactoring)
5. **[ArkTS Static Type Refactoring Best Practices](#5-arkts-static-type-refactoring-best-practices)** → Learn best practices for object conversion, ESValue, JSON, EAWorker, transfer, etc.
6. **[Appendix: Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)](#6-appendix-faq)** → Resolve common issues

### 1.3 Terminology

| English Term | Chinese Term | Brief Description |
|-------------|-------------|-------------------|
| **ArkTS-Dyn** | ArkTS动态类型模式 | ArkTS-Dyn runs in a dynamic typing mode. On the basis of retaining the basic style of TypeScript, it partially enhances concurrency capabilities and supports efficient interoperability with TypeScript and JavaScript; its object types are only used as a reference for static checking during compilation, and the binding of actual types occurs at runtime; objects created by default are still restricted by the thread isolation mechanism and cannot be shared directly. |
| **ArkTS-Sta** | ArkTS静态类型模式 | ArkTS-Sta runs in a static typing mode. The objects it creates have natural concurrent sharing capabilities, avoiding the thread isolation mechanism required for dynamically typed objects, making it suitable for multi-threaded shared access scenarios; the types of all its variables and expressions are determined at compile time (statically). Before generating executable code, the compiler performs strict type checking and does not allow type-mismatched assignments or operations. |
| **'use static'** | 静态类型声明标识 | For applications using hvigor as the build system, the following identifier must be added at the beginning of the file to indicate the use of ArkTS static type syntax. |
| **AOT** | Ahead-Of-Time编译 | Pre-compilation technology that converts bytecode to machine code at compile time to improve performance |
| **EAWorker** | Exclusive ArkTS Worker | Exclusive ArkTS worker thread supporting shared memory concurrency |
| **Interop** | 互操作能力 | The ability for ArkTS-Sta and ArkTS-Dyn to interact with each other |
| **ESValue** | 互操作接口 | Bridge interface for ArkTS-Sta and ArkTS-Dyn interoperability |
| **STValue** | 互操作接口 | Bridge interface for ArkTS-Dyn and ArkTS-Sta interoperability |
| **NAPI** | ArkTS-Dyn与C++跨语言交互接口 | Provides bidirectional interaction for ArkTS dynamic types and C++ code |
| **ANI** | ArkTS-Sta与C++跨语言交互接口 | Provides bidirectional interaction for ArkTS static types and C++ code |
| **Taihe** | 跨语言 API 编程模型 | Defines multi-language system interface programming models, providing simple and easy-to-use API publishing and consumption mechanisms |
| **IDL** | 接口定义语言 | Taihe uses Interface Definition Language to describe C++ API interfaces |
| **AKI** | 基于NAPI的跨语言开发框架 | ArkTS FFI development framework based on NAPI |
| **.ohidl** | Taihe IDL文件后缀 | Configured in the specified directory of the project. The Taihe compiler will parse this IDL file and generate interface code |


### 1.4 Constraints

- **Version Evolution**: ArkTS static types are continuously evolving. This document is written based on the current version, and some features may be adjusted in subsequent versions.
- **API Scope Description**: The static type SDK and application framework API scope described in this document is for APIs currently under development and will continue to expand in future versions.

---

## 🚀 2. ArkTS Static Types Overview

### 2.1 Static Type Refactoring Scenarios

#### 2.1.1 Core Value

**Performance Improvement**

- **Startup Acceleration**: AOT pre-compilation and parallelization of key modules
- **Execution Efficiency**: Direct execution of machine code improves runtime performance

**Concurrency Capabilities**

- **Memory Sharing**: EAWorker supports memory sharing between threads, breaking through message passing limitations
- **Zero-copy Transfer**: Directly shared objects without serialization overhead
- **Parallel Processing**: Multi-threaded parallel execution, releasing main thread resources

---

#### 2.1.2 Typical Application Scenarios

| Scenario Type | Typical Characteristics |
|:-------------|:------------------------|
| **Compute-intensive** | Data encryption/decryption, image processing, compression algorithms |
| **Startup Bottleneck** | Slow application startup, long initialization time |
| **High Concurrency Demand** | Tasks requiring multi-threaded parallel processing |
| **Performance-sensitive** | Perceptible stuttering and latency by users |

---

## 📚 3. Migrating Applications to ArkTS Static Types

### 3.1 Migration Refactoring Process Overview

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A("Start Refactoring") --> B["Scenario Identification<br/>1. Find modules to refactor"]
    B --> C["Scenario Identification<br/>2. Dependency analysis<br/>before module refactoring"]
    C --> D["Scenario Identification<br/>3. Performance benefit<br/>estimation after refactoring"]
    D --> E["Module Refactoring<br/>1. Refactored module<br/>architecture design"]
    E --> F["Module Refactoring<br/>2. Code refactoring<br/>best practices"]
    F --> G["Module Refactoring<br/>3. Refactor TS code to<br/>ArkTS dynamic code<br/>(as needed)"]
    G --> H["Module Refactoring<br/>4. ArkTS dynamic to static<br/>migration tool<br/>(syntax adaptation)"]
    H --> I["Module Refactoring<br/>5. ArkTS static code<br/>parallelization refactoring"]
    I --> J["Function Verification"]
    J --> K["Performance Acceptance<br/>1. Enable AOT"]
    K --> L["Performance Acceptance<br/>2. Performance verification"]
    L --> M("Refactoring Complete")

    style A fill:#D3D3D3
    style B fill:#ff7f0e
    style C fill:#ff7f0e
    style D fill:#ff7f0e
    style E fill:#2ca02c
    style F fill:#2ca02c
    style G fill:#2ca02c
    style H fill:#2ca02c
    style I fill:#2ca02c
    style J fill:#d62728
    style K fill:#9467bd
    style L fill:#9467bd
    style M fill:#D3D3D3
```

### 3.2 Scenario Identification

#### 3.2.1 Finding Modules to Refactor

##### 3.2.1.1 Using HiTrace for Time Consumption Analysis

Use HiTrace in business code to analyze key time-consuming points. For detailed usage, refer to the official documentation: [HiTrace](https://developer.huawei.com/consumer/cn/doc/harmonyos-guides/hitrace)

**Basic Usage**

```typescript
import hiTraceMeter from '@ohos.hiTraceMeter';

hiTraceMeter.startTrace("TaskTrace", 0x001);
...
const bundleInfo:bundleManager.BundleInfo = bundleManager.getBundleInfoForSelfSync(
      bundleManager.BundleFlag.GET_BUNDLE_INFO_WITH_APPLICATION |
      bundleManager.BundleFlag.GET_BUNDLE_INFO_WITH_METADATA
    );
...
hiTraceMeter.finishTrace("TaskTrace", 0x001);
```

**Common Time-consuming Scenarios**

| Scenario Type | Problem Manifestation |
|:--------------|:---------------------|
| Dynamic library loading time | Dynamic library file loading takes too long during application startup/module initialization |
| Business logic execution time | Single code segment execution takes too long in core processes (e.g., data processing, calculations, etc.) |

##### 3.2.1.2 Example Analysis

###### Step 1: Analyze Business Code Execution Flow

The following is an example business execution flow:

![原动态代码执行图](data:image/png;base64,)

**Example Code (TaskB+Data Initialization)**

```typescript
import lazy bundleManager from '@ohos.bundle.bundleManager';
import hiTraceMeter from '@ohos.hiTraceMeter';
import { Data } from './Data';

export function TaskB():void {
  // Trace start point
  hiTraceMeter.startTrace("Task-inner", 0x001);

  try {
    // Read bundleInfo information
    const bundleInfo:bundleManager.BundleInfo = bundleManager.getBundleInfoForSelfSync(
      bundleManager.BundleFlag.GET_BUNDLE_INFO_WITH_APPLICATION |
      bundleManager.BundleFlag.GET_BUNDLE_INFO_WITH_METADATA
    );

    const appInfo:bundleManager.ApplicationInfo = bundleInfo.appInfo;
    if (!appInfo?.metadataArray) {
      throw new Error("Invalid appInfo or no metadata found");
    }

    // Traverse data and store in Map
    for (const item of appInfo.metadataArray) {
      if (!item?.metadata) {
        continue;
      }
      for (const innerItem of item.metadata) {
        if (innerItem?.name && innerItem?.value) {
          Data.getInstance().addMetadata(innerItem.name, innerItem.value);
        }
      }
    }
  } catch (error) {
    hilog.error(DOMAIN, TASK, `Task error: ${error}`);
  } finally {
    // Trace end point
    hiTraceMeter.finishTrace("Task-inner", 0x001);
  }
}
```

###### Step 2: Analyze Trace

- **Task Total Time**: 7.9ms
  ![TaskTrace](data:image/png;base64,)

- **Loading libbundlemanager.so Time**: 1.7ms
  ![加载libbundlemanager.so](data:image/png;base64,)

###### Step 3: Conclusion

In this example, TaskA takes 10ms, TaskC takes 1ms. Based on the above information, we can conclude:

| Item | Time |
|------|------|
| TaskA | 10ms |
| TaskB+Data Parsing | 7.9ms |
| TaskC | 1ms |
| **Total** | **18.9ms** |

**Conclusion: TaskB+Data parsing execution takes 7.9ms, accounting for 42% of the main thread's total time of 18.9ms. Parallelization refactoring yields significant benefits.**


#### 3.2.2 Dependency Analysis Before Module Refactoring

Before determining the refactoring plan, analyze the module's call chain and dependency relationships to assess the refactoring scope and feasibility.

##### 3.2.2.1 Existing Module Execution Flow Analysis

**Existing Problems**

- **Main Thread Blocking**: Data initialization TaskB function needs to execute sequentially on the main thread
- **Resource Occupation**: Long occupation of main thread resources affects system response efficiency

##### 3.2.2.2 Parallelization Refactoring Plan

| Refactoring Dimension | Refactoring Strategy | Expected Effect |
|:---------------------|:---------------------|:----------------|
| **Concurrency Optimization** | Migrate data initialization logic to child threads for asynchronous execution | Release main thread to handle other business |
| **Memory Sharing** | Refactor TaskB and Data functions to static code | Achieve efficient sharing between threads, avoid repeated loading, ensure thread-safe access |


#### 3.2.3 Performance Benefit Estimation After Refactoring

##### 3.2.3.1 Parallelized Execution Flow

After parallelization refactoring, TaskA and TaskB can execute in parallel, and TaskC waits for both to complete before executing:

![改造后混编代码执行流程图](data:image/png;base64,)

##### 3.2.3.2 Performance Benefit Comparison

| Plan | Execution Method | Total Time |
|:-----|:----------------|:-----------|
| Original Plan | Single-threaded serial execution | TaskA:10ms + TaskB:7.9ms + TaskC:1ms = 18.9ms |
| Parallelization Refactoring | TaskA and TaskB execute in parallel | TaskA:10ms + TaskC:1ms = 11ms|

**Conclusion: TaskB+Data parsing execution parallelization refactoring is expected to yield benefits.**


### 3.3 Module Refactoring

#### 3.3.1 Refactored Module Architecture Design

When refactoring a specific module, consider the following aspects:
* **The module can be compatible with new and old devices**, meaning the refactored application package can be distributed to all devices;
* **The module implementation supports runtime rollback**, meaning if there are bugs in the refactored static type implementation, it can be rolled back to the pre-refactoring implementation through a switch;
* **The module maintains unchanged external interfaces**, meaning external interfaces remain dynamic type unchanged.

Based on the above design goals, the abstract refactored module architecture design is shown below:

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="改造模块架构设计" title="改造模块架构设计" style="zoom:60%;" />

The module to be refactored mainly contains 1 interface file `Index.ets` and 2 feature implementations `dFeature1.ets` and `dFeature2.ets`. The goal is to add a static implementation `sFeature1.ets` for feature 1, and it can also run on devices that do not support static types. The overall architecture is divided into four layers:

- Interface layer: `Index.ets` needs to remain externally unchanged
- Dispatch layer: `dispatch.js` dispatches to static implementation or dynamic implementation at runtime based on API version number
- Proxy layer: `proxy.ets` is the proxy file for the static implementation `sFeature1.ets` of feature 1, to maintain external dynamic files
- Implementation layer: `sFeature1.ets` business logic implementation, generally undergoes concurrency refactoring

In the ArkTS static type refactoring case [Single Codebase Supporting ArkTS Dynamic/Static Types](###41-single-codebase-supporting-arkts-dynamicstatic-types), the writing methods of each layer will be shown in detail with code.

#### 3.3.2 Code Refactoring Best Practices

Based on the refactored module architecture, the following code refactoring best practices are summarized:

- **Static calling dynamic implementation**: The business side defines subclasses with dynamic open APIs. In static context, these objects can only be accessed after returning from child threads to the main thread
- **Dispatch layer implementation**: Externally provided APIs may be called by child threads and need to be static, while defining dispatch classes properly
- **Proxy layer implementation**: Needs to accept dynamic type parameters and access their properties or methods. If dynamic and static 2 classes need to implement consistently, proxy classes need to be defined to implement dynamic to static type conversion

#### 3.3.3 Refactoring TS Code to ArkTS Dynamic Code (As Needed)

For detailed TypeScript to ArkTS dynamic code refactoring guide, please refer to:

📖 **[TypeScript to ArkTS Code Refactoring Guide](https://developer.huawei.com/consumer/cn/doc/harmonyos-guides/typescript-to-arkts-migration-guide)**

#### 3.3.4 ArkTS Dynamic to Static Migration Tool

EasyTrans is the official migration tool for ArkTS dynamic types to static types, integrated in the IDE, providing code scanning and automatic syntax rule fixing functions, which can significantly reduce migration costs.


##### File Scanning

**Opening the Migration Tool**

Open the EasyTrans migration tool interface in the IDE:

![迁移工具使用](data:image/png;base64,)

**Selecting Scan Scope**

| Scan Scope | Applicable Scenarios |
|:-----------|:---------------------|
| **Project Level** | Overall for migration, recommended for first scan |
| **Folder Level** | For specific module migration |
| **Single File Level** | Verify refactoring effect of individual files |

**Selecting Scan Mode**

| Scan Type | Scan Speed | Supports Interop Issue Scanning | Applicable Scenarios |
|:----------|:-----------|:---------------------------------|:---------------------|
| **Fast Scan** | ⚡ Fast | ❌ No | Quick assessment, preliminary investigation |
| **Full Scan** | 🔍 Complete | ✅ Yes | Comprehensive check before formal migration |

> **Recommendation**: For first migration, use **Full Scan** mode.

##### Issue Fixing

After scanning, the tool displays all fixable syntax rule code:

![扫描结果](data:image/png;base64,)

**Issue Classification**

| Issue Type | Identifier | Handling Method |
|:-----------|:-----------|:----------------|
| **Auto-fixable** | Auto-fixable | Click "Fix All" button in the upper right corner |
| **Manually-fixable** | Manually-fixable | View details on the right, modify manually according to suggestions |

**Operation Steps**

1. **Auto-fix Issues**
   - Check the issues to be fixed
   - Click the **"Fix All"** or **"Fix Selected"** button in the toolbar
   - The tool automatically completes fixing and displays the results

2. **Manually Fix Issues**
   - Click on the issue item, the right panel displays:
     - Issue description
     - Modification suggestions
     - Code examples
   - Manually adjust code according to prompts

##### Usage Recommendations

| Scenario | Recommended Operation |
|:---------|:----------------------|
| **First Migration** | Use Full Scan for comprehensive scanning → Batch auto-fix → Process manual issues item by item |
| **Incremental Updates** | Use Fast Scan for quick verification of new code |
| **Complex Projects** | Scan and fix by modules to avoid excessive one-time changes |

#### 3.3.5 Migrating C++ Code from NAPI to ANI

C++ code migration mainly includes the following three schemes:

| Migration Scenario | Description |
|-------------------|-------------|
| **NAPI → ANI** | Migrate directly from Node-API to ANI |
| **NAPI → Taihe** | Use Taihe tool to automatically generate bridge code |
| **AKI → Taihe** | Migrate from AKI framework to Taihe framework code |


##### Scheme 1. NAPI → ANI

Applicable scenario: Small project scale, simple interfaces

| Content | Link |
|---------|------|
| Migration Guide | [Node-API to ANI Migration Guide](https://gitcode.com/openharmony/docs/blob/OpenHarmony_feature_20250702/zh-cn/application-dev/napi/napi2ani/napi2ani_guide.md) |
| ANI Complete Manual | [Ark Native Interface Application Practice Manual](https://gitcode.com/openharmony/docs/blob/OpenHarmony_feature_20250702/zh-cn/application-dev/ani/ani-usage-scenarios.md) |

**Core Differences**:
- **Module Loading**: NAPI automatic registration → ANI explicit `loadLibrary()`
- **Type System**: Dynamic type `napi_value` → Static type `ani_int`/`ani_object`, etc.
- **Mangling Rules**: Need to write method signatures such as `"ii:"` (two int parameters)

##### Scheme 2. NAPI → Taihe

Applicable scenario: Large project scale, complex interfaces

| Content | Link |
|---------|------|
| Migration Guide | [Node-API to Taihe Migration Guide](https://gitcode.com/openharmony/docs/blob/OpenHarmony_feature_20250702/zh-cn/application-dev/napi/napi2ani/napi2taihe.md) |
| Taihe Introduction | [What is Taihe?](https://gitcode.com/openharmony/docs/blob/OpenHarmony_feature_20250702/zh-cn/application-dev/taihe/taihe-introduction.md) |
| Quick Start | [Taihe Quick Start](https://gitcode.com/openharmony/docs/blob/OpenHarmony_feature_20250702/zh-cn/application-dev/taihe/taihe-quick-start.md) |
| IDL Syntax | [Taihe IDL Reference](https://gitcode.com/openharmony/docs/blob/OpenHarmony_feature_20250702/zh-cn/application-dev/taihe/taihe-idl-reference.md) |

**Migration Steps**:
1. Write `.ohidl` interface definition file

- Based on NAPI business declaration file content as follows:
```typescript
export const add: (a: number, b: number) => number;
```

- Corresponding ohidl interface definition file content as follows:
```idl
function add(a: f64, b: f64) => f64;
```
Here a, b, add return value types need to be adjusted according to actual situations, for example, if explicitly integer, i32, i64 can be used

- ohidl file placement location as follows:

![ohidl文件放置位置](data:image/png;base64,)

1. Execute compilation to generate glue code

- Execute compilation operation on the module where the ohidl file is located

![napi2taihe模块编译](data:image/png;base64,)

- Check if there are ohidl file errors in compilation results

![ohidl编译报错处理](data:image/png;base64,)

- After resolving ohidl file errors, find the temp folder in the module's build/default/generated/ohidl/default directory. The ani_constructor.cpp file in it is the Taihe business module registration file. The other file with .impl.cpp suffix is the interface implementation file corresponding to the ohidl definition file. How many ohidl files there are will generate how many .impl.cpp files. Copy the temp folder to the module source code directory

![Taihe胶水代码拷贝](data:image/png;base64,)

- Add the following to the cmake packaging configuration file:
```cmake
include(${OHIDL_GENERATED_CMAKE_FILE})
include_directories(
  ${TAIHE_GEN_INCLUDE}
  ${TAIHE_RUNTIME_INCLUDE}
)
add_library(yourLibName SHARED
  ${TAIHE_GEN_SRC}
  ${TAIHE_RUNTIME_SRC}
)
```

1. Implement C++ Business Logic

- Edit interface implementation file
```cpp
#include "calc.impl.hpp"
#include "stdexcept"

namespace {
// Interface implementation
double add(double a, double b) {
    return a + b;
}
}  // namespace

// Since these macros are auto-generate, lint will cause false positive.
// NOLINTBEGIN
// Interface binding
TH_EXPORT_CPP_API_add(add);
// NOLINTEND
```

4. Interface Call in ArkTS Logic

- Load so
ANI adopts explicit loading scheme, allowing loadLibrary to be used for loading when so interface needs to be called at runtime
```typescript
loadLibrary('yourLibName');
```

- Interface call
```typescript
// Method import needs to import the ets file generated in the build folder
// Example uses relative path import, please modify relative path according to actual scenario
import { add } from '../../../build/default/generated/ohidl/default/calc';
const ret = add(1, 2);
```

**Notes**:
1. The temp folder is just a template folder. When familiar with Taihe registration rules and syntax, you can manually write business module registration and business interface implementation files without manual copying;
2. During the migration process, if you need to modify ohidl after some C++ has been implemented, the re-generated business interface implementation file needs to be manually modified. For example, if an interface is added in ohidl, you need to manually copy the interface declaration in the temp folder to the interface implementation file that has already implemented some functions.

##### Scheme 3. AKI → Taihe

Applicable scenario: Projects already using AKI framework

| Content | Link |
|---------|------|
| Migration Guide | [AKI to Taihe Migration Guide](https://gitcode.com/openharmony/docs/blob/OpenHarmony_feature_20250702/zh-cn/application-dev/napi/napi2ani/aki2taihe.md) |

#### 3.3.6: ArkTS Static Code Parallelization Refactoring

After completing the ArkTS dynamic to static syntax migration, the next step is to parallelize the code to fully utilize multi-core CPU capabilities and improve application performance. ArkTS static types provide two main parallelization schemes: **taskpool** (task pool) and **EAWorker** (exclusive thread task executor).

##### 3.3.6.1 taskpool Parallelization Refactoring

taskpool is a task pool mechanism provided by ArkTS static types, used to manage multiple worker threads and automatically assign tasks to available threads for execution, suitable for executing a large number of short-term tasks.

**Core Features**:
- Automatic thread management: No need to care about thread lifecycle
- Task priority: Supports four priorities: HIGH, MEDIUM, LOW, IDLE
- Task group: Supports batch execution of a group of related tasks
- Serial queue: Supports task queues that need sequential execution
- Memory sharing: Natively supports memory sharing without needing @Sendable decorator

**Applicable Scenarios**:
- Large number of independent computing tasks
- Data processing, encryption/decryption and other CPU-intensive operations
- Multi-task scenarios requiring concurrent execution

**[taskpool Reference Documentation](https://gitcode.com/openharmony/docs/blob/OpenHarmony_feature_20250702/zh-cn/application-dev/reference/native-lib/arkts-sta-taskpool.md)**

##### 3.3.6.2 EAWorker Parallelization Refactoring

EAWorker (Exclusive ArkTS Worker) is an exclusive thread task executor that provides developers with dedicated worker threads, suitable for executing long-term tasks or tasks that need exclusive resources.

**Core Features**:
- Exclusive thread: Each EAWorker instance binds to a dedicated thread
- Thread reuse: Can reuse the same EAWorker instance to execute multiple tasks
- Interop support: Can optionally enable interop capabilities with ArkTS-Dyn
- Lifecycle management: Needs to manually call join to release resources

**Applicable Scenarios**:
- Long-running tasks (e.g., data synchronization, background processing)
- Tasks that need exclusive thread resources
- Scenarios requiring interop with ArkTS-Dyn code

**[EAWorker Reference Documentation](https://gitcode.com/openharmony/docs/blob/OpenHarmony_feature_20250702/zh-cn/application-dev/reference/native-lib/eaworker_managed.md)**

#### 3.3.7 Function Verification

After completing ArkTS static refactoring and parallelization refactoring, comprehensive function verification of the refactored code is required to ensure:
- The refactored static code functions are consistent with the original dynamic code
- Parallelization refactoring execution results are correct
- Dynamic-static interop works normally
- Exception handling mechanisms are effective


### 3.4 Performance Acceptance

After completing code refactoring, AOT compilation needs to be enabled and performance verified to ensure static refactoring achieves expected results.

#### 3.4.1 Enabling AOT


##### 3.4.1.1 Manually Enabling AOT

**Manually Triggering AOT Compilation on Device**

Manually trigger application AOT compilation on a real device or simulator via hdc command:

```bash
# 1. Connect device
hdc shell

# 2. Disable SELinux (temporary)
setenforce 0

# 3. Execute full mode AOT compilation
bm compile -m full "com.example.yourapp"

# Or use hdc to execute directly (without entering shell)
hdc shell setenforce 0 && bm compile -m full "com.example.yourapp"
```

**Command Description**

| Parameter | Description |
|:----------|:------------|
| `hdc shell` | Enter device shell environment |
| `setenforce 0` | Temporarily disable SELinux (required for compilation) |
| `bm compile` | Bundle Manager compilation command |
| `-m full` | Full compilation mode, execute AOT |
| `"Package Name"` | Application package name |

**Getting Application Package Name**

```bash
# Method 1: View installed application list
hdc shell bm list -a

# Method 2: View from app.json5
{
  "bundleName": "com.example.yourapp"
}
```

---

#### 3.4.2 Performance Verification

##### 3.4.2.1 Key Performance Indicators

Runtime performance verification needs to focus on the following core indicators:

| Indicator | Measurement Method |
|:----------|:-------------------|
| **Cold Startup Time** | From startup to homepage interactive |
| **Key Path Time** | Measure using HiTrace |
| **Memory Usage** | DevEco Studio Profiler|
| **CPU Utilization** | Profiler performance analysis |

##### 3.4.2.2 Startup Time Measurement

```typescript
'use static'

import hiTraceMeter from '@ohos.hiTraceMeter';

export class PerformanceMonitor {
  static measureLaunchTime(): void {
    // Start timing when application starts
    hiTraceMeter.startTrace("AppLaunch", 0x001);

    // Homepage rendering complete
    hiTraceMeter.finishTrace("AppLaunch", 0x001);

    // View "AppLaunch" time in Trace
  }
}
```

**Key Path Time Measurement**

Use HiTrace to mark key paths before and after refactoring:

```typescript
'use static'

import hiTraceMeter from '@ohos.hiTraceMeter';

export function TaskB(): void {
  hiTraceMeter.startTrace("TaskB-Static", 0x002);

  try {
    // Business logic
  } finally {
    hiTraceMeter.finishTrace("TaskB-Static", 0x002);
  }
}

// Comparison indicators
// Dynamic type TaskB time: 7.9ms
// Static type TaskB time: 5.2ms (-34%)
```

##### 3.4.2.3 Using Profiling Tools

**[DevEco Profiler Optimization Tool Introduction](https://developer.huawei.com/consumer/cn/doc/harmonyos-guides/ide-profiler)**

---

## 🛠️ 4. ArkTS Static Type Migration Cases

### 4.1 Single Codebase Supporting ArkTS Dynamic/Static Types

This section uses the open-source sample project **ArkTS-Sta** as a case to explain how to support both ArkTS dynamic types (dyn) and static types (sta) in the same set of business code, and gradually complete the migration from dynamic implementation to static implementation.

**Sample Project Address**:

- Repository: <https://gitcode.com/kkup180/arkts-sta>
- Documentation: <https://gitcode.com/kkup180/arkts-sta/blob/main/README.md>

#### 4.1.1 Case Objectives

This project focuses on the following three objectives:

1. **Unified Business Interface**: Expose only one set of `DataParser` / `DataSource` / `CardData` interfaces externally, business layer is unaware.
2. **Dual Implementation Coexistence**: Retain both dyn (dynamic) and sta (static) implementations in the library.
3. **Runtime Dispatch**: Automatically route to dyn or sta via `dispatch` layer based on `sdkApiVersion`.

This is a typical "**compatibility first, replacement later**" migration path, suitable for smooth evolution of existing applications.

#### 4.1.2 Project Structure and Responsibility Division

Core directories can be abstracted as follows:

```text
entry (Business Call Layer)
  └── pages/Index.ets

library (Capability Implementation Layer)
  ├── components/Index.ets                 # Unified export
  ├── components/dispatch/DataParser.js    # Version dispatch
  ├── components/dyn/*                     # Dynamic implementation
  ├── components/sta/*                     # Static implementation
  ├── components/sta_wrapper/*             # sta adaptation to dyn interfaces
  ├── components/sta_bridge/*              # TS/ETS bridging and static instance initialization
  └── components/sta_dummy/*               # Static fallback empty implementation
```

Responsibility description of each layer:

- **entry layer**: Only calls unified interfaces, does not directly depend on dyn/sta details.
- **dispatch layer**: Responsible for runtime judgment and selection of specific implementation.
- **dyn/sta implementation layer**: Respectively carry dynamic and static code logic, maintaining capability consistency.
- **wrapper/bridge layer**: Solve key issues in the migration process such as "how static implementation adapts to dynamic interfaces" and "how TS calls ETS".

#### 4.1.3 Key Refactoring Path from Dynamic to Static

##### Step 1: First Converge Interfaces, Then Introduce Static Implementation

Before migration, a common problem is that the business side directly depends on specific implementations. This case first converges capabilities to a unified export (`components/Index.ets`), and business only depends on abstract interfaces.

**Refactoring Principle**: First do "interface unification", then do "implementation replacement".

##### Step 2: Implement Isomorphic Capabilities

Without removing the dyn implementation, supplement the sta version's isomorphic capabilities (such as `DataParser`, `DataSource`, `CardData`, `ResolverRegistry`).

**Goal**: Ensure dyn/sta directories maintain consistency in input/output models, avoiding business layer branching judgments.

##### Step 3: Add Dispatch Version Dispatch

Route via `dispatch/DataParser.js` based on system API version:

- `sdkApiVersion >= 23`: Go to static implementation wrapper (such as `DataParserStaW`)
- `sdkApiVersion < 23`: Go to dynamic implementation (such as `DataParserDyn`)

This can achieve "automatically select optimal implementation based on device capability" in the same version application.

##### Step 4: Solve Interop via wrapper + bridge

After introducing static implementation, the following problems are usually encountered:

- Business interfaces are still dyn style, sta needs to adapt input/output;
- TS and ArkTS cannot directly call seamlessly, need bridging initialization;
- In some scenarios, fallback empty implementation is needed to ensure link availability.

This case forms a deployable migration "intermediate layer" solution through the cooperation of `sta_wrapper`, `sta_bridge`, `sta_dummy` three layers.

#### 4.1.5 Runtime Chain (From Call to Rendering)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[entry page calls DataParser] --> B[library unified export]
    B --> C[dispatch version judgment]
    C -->|sdkApiVersion < 23| D[dyn DataParser]
    C -->|sdkApiVersion >= 23| E[sta_wrapper DataParserStaW]
    E --> F[sta_bridge initialization/bridging]
    F --> G[sta DataParser.ets]
    D --> H[unified CardData model]
    G --> H
    H --> I[entry render list]
```

This chain reflects reflects the core of migration design: **implementation replaceable, interface unchanged, business low awareness**.

#### 4.1.6 How to Reuse This Case in Your Project

Can land in the following order:

1. **Identify Hot Modules**: Prioritize modules that can obtain obvious benefits such as parsing, calculation, long chains.
2. **Abstract Unified Interfaces**: First converge business calls to a single export.
3. **Maintain Dual Implementations in Parallel**: Retain dyn, add sta isomorphic implementation.
4. **Add Dispatch Dispatch**: First divert by API version, can subsequently extend to grayscale switch.
5. **Supplement Adaptation and Bridging Layers**: Handle dyn/sta data structure conversion.
6. **Unified Acceptance Criteria**: Simultaneously verify functional consistency and performance benefits.

#### 4.1.7 Case Value Summary

The value of this project is not in "replacing a certain file", but in providing a replicable methodology:

- **Architecture**: Unified interface + dual implementation + runtime dispatch;
- **Engineering**: wrapper/bridge/dummy layered decoupling;
- **Migration**: Supports gradual evolution, controllable risk, clear rollback path.

For existing ArkTS-Dyn applications, this is a realistic and feasible static refactoring path.

### 4.2 ArkUI Static Type Parallelization Refactoring

For application developers, UI rendering performance is one of the key factors affecting user experience. ArkTS static types provide UI parallelization creation capabilities, allowing the component tree creation process to migrate from the main thread to child threads, thereby reducing main thread load and reducing page rendering latency. This section introduces how to refactor existing ArkTS dynamic type applications to use parallelized UI static type applications.

#### 4.2.1 Refactoring Overview

##### 4.2.1.1 Value of Parallelization Refactoring

Traditional UI rendering adopts single-threaded serial creation, and as page structure becomes increasingly complex, it gradually becomes a performance bottleneck. ArkTS static types' UI parallelization capabilities can bring the following benefits:

| Benefit Type | Specific Manifestation |
|:-------------|:-----------------------|
| **Reduce Main Thread Load** | Disperse complex UI creation tasks to child threads, releasing main thread resources for user interaction processing |
| **Reduce Page Rendering Latency** | Parallel creation can proceed simultaneously with other main thread operations, improving page response speed |
| **Improve Application Smoothness** | Reduce stuttering caused by UI creation, improving user experience |
| **Support Complex Page Construction** | Performance improvement is more obvious for complex pages containing a large number of components |

##### 4.2.1.2 Applicable Scenarios for Parallelization Refactoring

| Scenario Type | Applicability Description | Refactoring Benefit |
|:-------------|:-------------------------|:---------------------|
| **Large Complex Pages** | Pages contain a large number of components (>50), single creation takes long time | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Off-screen Content** | Content that can be displayed with delay, areas that need scrolling to see | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **List/Grid Long Lists** | Lists or grid containers containing a large number of sub-items | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Page Switching Scenarios** | Hope to reduce page jump response latency | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Simple UI Interaction** | Simple pages containing only a small number of components | ⭐ |

##### 4.2.1.3 API Methods for Parallelization Refactoring

ArkTS static types provide the following parallelization API methods:

**Declarative Parallelization - ParallelizeUI**

| API Interface | Description | Applicable Scenarios |
|:-------------|:------------|:---------------------|
| `ParallelizeUI(options, content_)` | Basic declarative parallel creation method | Simple UI parallel creation not depending on external state variables |
| `ParallelizeUI<T>(options, param, content_)` | Parallel creation method supporting state variable passing | Parallel creation scenarios needing to use external state variables |
| `ParallelizeUI<V, T>(options, arr, param, content_)` | Parallel loop creation method | Parallel creation scenarios for List/Grid long list sub-items |

**Imperative Parallelization - BuilderNode**

| API Capability | Description |Applicable Scenarios |
|:--------------|:------------|:---------------------|
| `build(builder, params, { useParallel: true })` | BuilderNode parallel build capability | Complex UI scenarios for imperative node tree creation |

#### 4.2.2 ParallelizeUI Parallelization Refactoring

##### 4.2.2.1 ParallelizeUI Introduction

`ParallelizeUI` is a declarative parallelization creation method. Its internal UI is created in child threads. After creation is complete, it returns to the main thread to complete tree mounting. Subsequent updates, events, and other operations are still performed in the main thread.

**Working Principle**:
```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Main thread call] --> B[Dispatch to child thread]
    B --> C[Child thread creates component tree]
    C --> D[Return to main thread]
    D --> E[Mount to UI tree]
    E --> F[Subsequent updates in main thread]
```

##### 4.2.2.2 Basic Usage Refactoring

**Original Code (Serial Creation)**:
```ts
// ArkTS-Dyn example
import { Entry, Text, Column, Component, Button, List, ListItem } from '@ohos.arkui.component';
import { State } from '@ohos.arkui.stateManagement';

@Entry
@Component
struct Index {
  @State count: number = 0;
  @State listData: string[] = ['项目1', '项目2', '项目3'];

  build() {
    Column() {
      // Serial creation, all components created in main thread
      Row() {
        Text(this.count.toString())
          .fontSize(24)
      }

      List({ space: 8 }) {
        ForEach(this.listData, (item: string) => {
          ListItem() {
            Text(item).fontSize(16)
          }
        })
      }
    }
  }
}
```

**Refactored Code (Parallel Creation)**:
```ts
'use static'

import { Entry, Text, Column, Component, Button, List, ListItem, Row } from '@ohos.arkui.component';
import { State } from '@ohos.arkui.stateManagement';
import { ParallelizeUI } from '@ohos.arkui.Parallelize';

@Entry
@Component
struct Index {
  @State count: int = 0;
  @State listData: string[] = ['项目1', '项目2', '项目3'];

  build() {
    Column() {
      // Use ParallelizeUI to parallel create Row component
      ParallelizeUI(undefined) {
        Row() {
          Text(this.count.toString())
            .fontSize(24)
        }
      }

      // Use ParallelizeUI to parallel create List component
      ParallelizeUI({ enable: true }) {
        List({ space: 8 }) {
          ForEach(this.listData, (item: string) => {
            ListItem() {
              Text(item).fontSize(16)
            }
          })
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

##### 4.2.2.3 State Variable Passing Refactoring

In parallelization environment, cannot directly use externally defined state variables (such as `@State`, `@Link`, `@PropRef`, etc.), need to use `ParallelizeUI<T>` overload interface to pass via parameters.

**Error Usage (Will cause runtime exception)**:
```ts
'use static'

import { Entry, Text, Column, Component } from '@ohos.arkui.component';
import { State } from '@ohos.arkui.stateManagement';
import { ParallelizeUI } from '@ohos.arkui.Parallelize';

@Entry
@Component
struct Index {
  @State str: string = 'Hello';

  build() {
    Column() {
      // ❌ Error: Cannot directly use external state variables inside ParallelizeUI
      ParallelizeUI(undefined) {
        Text(this.str)  // Will trigger runtime exception in Debug mode
          .fontSize(50)
      }
    }
  }
}
```

**Correct Usage (Pass via Parameters)**:
```ts
'use static'

import { Entry, Text, Column, Component, Button, ClickEvent } from '@ohos.arkui.component';
import { State } from '@ohos.arkui.stateManagement';
import { ParallelizeUI } from '@ohos.arkui.Parallelize';

// Encapsulate parameter class
class Param {
  str: string;
  constructor(str: string) {
    this.str = str;
  }
}

@Entry
@Component
struct Index {
  @State str: string = 'Hello';

  build() {
    Column() {
      // ✅ Correct: Use ParallelizeUI<T> to pass state variables via parameters
      ParallelizeUI<Param>(undefined,
        () => { return new Param(this.str); },  // Construct parameters in main thread
        (param: Param) => {                     // Use parameters in child thread
          Text(param.str)
            .fontSize(50)
        }
      )

      Button('UpperCase')
        .onClick((event: ClickEvent) => {
          this.str = this.str.toUpperCase()  // After state variable updates, parallel UI will recreate in next frame
        })
    }
  }
}
```

##### 4.2.2.4 List&Grid Parallelization Refactoring

`ParallelizeUI` provides loop creation overload interface `ParallelizeUI<V, T>` for parallelizing List and Grid sub-component creation.

**Before Refactoring (Serial Create List List Items)**:
```ts
// ArkTS-Dyn or ArkTS-Sta (serial)
List({ space: 10 }) {
  ForEach(this.videoList, (item: VideoInfo) => {
    ListItem() {
      VideoCard({ info: item })
    }
  })
}
```

**After Refactoring (Parallel Create List List Items)**:
```ts
'use static'

import { Entry, Text, Column, Component, List, ListItem, Image, Row, Stack, $r, ImageFit, Alignment, FontWeight, TextOverflow } from '@ohos.arkui.component';
import { State } from '@ohos.arkui.stateManagement';
import { ParallelizeUI } from '@ohos.arkui.Parallelize';

// Video info class
class Info {
  title: string
  up: string
  views: string
  likes: string
  coverRes: string
  constructor(title: string, up: string, views: string, likes: string, coverRes: string) {
    this.title = title
    this.up = up
    this.views = views
    this.likes = likes
    this.coverRes = coverRes
  }
}

@Entry
@Component
struct Index {
  @State arr: Array<Int> = [1];
  @State stateVar: string = 'state var';

  aboutToAppear() {
    for (let i = 2; i <= 50; i++) {
      this.arr.push(i)
    }
  }

  build() {
    Column() {
      List({ space: 10 }) {
        // When used in List container, only parallel create currently visible nodes on demand
        ParallelizeUI<Int, Info>({ enable: true }, this.arr,
          (item: Int, index: Int) => {
            const coverIndex = ((item - 1) % 5) + 1
            const coverResId = `app.media.cover${coverIndex}`
            return new Info(
              `【热门】搞笑视频 ${item}`,
              `UP主：用户${item}`,
              `${(Math.random() * 100).toFixed(1)}万播放`,
              `${(Math.random() * 1).toFixed(1)}万点赞`,
              coverResId
            )
          },
          (param: Info) => {
            ListItem() {
              Column() {
                // Cover
                Stack() {
                  Image($r(param.coverRes))
                    .width('100%')
                    .height(200)
                    .borderRadius(8)
                    .objectFit(ImageFit.Cover)
                    .alt('video cover')

                  // Play count bottom right floating layer
                  Row() {
                    Text(param.views)
                      .fontSize(12)
                      .fontColor('#FFFFFF')
                      .backgroundColor('rgba(0,0,0,0.5)')
                      .borderRadius(10)
                  }
                  .align(Alignment.BottomEnd)
                }

                // Title
                Text(param.title)
                  .fontSize(18)
                  .fontWeight(FontWeight.Medium)
                  .maxLines(2)
                  .textOverflow({ overflow: TextOverflow.Ellipsis })

                // Bottom info row (up host, like count)
                Row() {
                  Text(param.up)
                    .fontSize(14)
                    .fontColor('#888')

                  Text(` · ${param.likes}`)
                    .fontSize(14)
                    .fontColor('#888')
                }
              }
              .backgroundColor('#FFFFFF')
              .borderRadius(12)
              .padding(8)
            }
            .width('100%')
          }
        )
      }
      .width('100%')
      .height('100%')
      .padding(10)
      .backgroundColor('#F8F8F8')
    }
  }
}
```

**Characteristics of Parallelized List/Grid**:
- **On-demand Creation**: Only parallel create nodes within currently visible area
- **Automatic Recycling**: Nodes automatically release after sliding out of visible area
- **Scrolling Optimization**: Parallel create nodes about to display during scrolling process

#### 4.2.3 BuilderNode Parallelization Refactoring

##### 4.2.3.1 BuilderNode Parallelization Introduction

`BuilderNode` provides imperative node creation capabilities, supporting complex UI creation and updates in child threads. Its parallelization capabilities are applicable to:

- Building/updating complex UI with a large number of nodes
- Reducing response latency during page switching
- After data loading completes, start building directly in child threads without waiting for main thread idle

##### 4.2.3.2 Refactoring Method

`BuilderNode` parallelization refactoring is very simple, just pass `useParallel: true` parameter when calling `build` method:

**Before Refactoring (Serial Build)**:
```ts
// Serial build
let builderNode = new BuilderNode<Params>(uiContext, renderOptions);
builderNode.build(wrapBuilder(MyBuilder), new Params("data"));  // Default serial
```

**After Refactoring (Parallel Build)**:
```ts
// Parallel build
let builderNode = new BuilderNode<Params>(uiContext, renderOptions);
builderNode.build(
  wrapBuilder(MyBuilder),
  new Params("data"),
  { useParallel: true }  // Enable parallel build
);
```

##### 4.2.3.3 Complete Refactoring Example

```ts
'use static'

import {
  Builder,
  Button,
  Column,
  Component,
  Entry,
  NodeContainer,
  wrapBuilder,
  Text,
  UIContext,
} from '@ohos.arkui.component';
import {
  BuilderNode,
  NodeController,
  FrameNode,
  NodeRenderType,
  RenderOptions,
  Size,
} from '@ohos.arkui.node';

// Custom parameters
class Params {
  text: string;
  constructor(text: string) {
    this.text = text;
  }
}

// Builder function
@Builder
function MyBuilder(params: Params) {
  Column() {
    Text(params.text).fontSize(20)
    Text("This is content created by BuilderNode")
  }
  .width('100%')
  .height(100)
}

class MyNodeController extends NodeController {
  private builderNode?: BuilderNode<Params>;
  private uiContext?: UIContext;

  makeNode(uiContext: UIContext): FrameNode | null {
    this.uiContext = uiContext;

    let renderOptions: RenderOptions = {
      selfIdealSize: { width: 100, height: 100 } as Size,
      type: NodeRenderType.RENDER_TYPE_DISPLAY
    };

    let builderNode:BuilderNode = new BuilderNode<Params>(uiContext, renderOptions);

    // Parallel execute build
    builderNode.build(
      wrapBuilder(MyBuilder),
      new Params("Parallel build"),
      { useParallel: true }  // ✅ Key: Enable parallel build
    );

    this.builderNode = builderNode;
    return builderNode.getFrameNode()!;
  }

  updateNode() {
    // If node not yet mounted, update operations will also execute in parallel
    this.builderNode?.update(new Params("Update data"));
  }

  dispose() {
    this.builderNode?.dispose();
    this.builderNode = undefined;
  }
}

@Entry
@Component
struct Page {
  private nodeController: MyNodeController = new MyNodeController();

  build() {
    Column() {
      NodeContainer(this.nodeController)
        .width('100%')
        .height(200)

      Button("Update Content")
        .onClick(() => {
          this.nodeController.updateNode();
        })
    }
  }
}
```

#### 4.2.4 Constraints and Limitations of Parallelization Refactoring

##### 4.2.4.1 State Variable Usage Constraints

| Constraint Type | Description | Solution |
|:----------------|:------------|:----------|
| **Prohibit Using External State Variables** | `@State`, `@Link`, `@PropRef`, `@Consumer`, `@StorageLink`, `@StoragePropRef`, `@LocalStorageLink`, `@LocalStoragePropRef` cannot be directly used in `ParallelizeUI` | Use `ParallelizeUI<T>` to pass via parameters |
| **Ordinary Variables Need Thread Safety** | Ordinary variables can be used in multi-threads, but need to ensure read-write safety | Use concurrent containers or locks for protection |

##### 4.2.4.2 Component Support Constraints

Currently `ParallelizeUI` does not support the following components, using them will trigger runtime errors:

| Unsupported Components | Alternative Solution |
|:----------------------|:---------------------|
| `Web` | No parallelization solution available |
| `RichText` | Use `Text` component instead |
| `WithTheme` | Use outside parallel area |

##### 4.2.4.3 Performance Constraints

| Constraint Item | Description | Suggestion |
|:----------------|:------------|:------------|
| **Task Scheduling Overhead** | Each parallel task has scheduling overhead | Suggest component count in each task > 50 |
| **Display Latency** | Parallel created UI displays in next frame | Suitable for content with delayed display |
| **Updates Still in Main Thread** | Post-creation update operations still in main thread | Focus on optimizing creation phase |

#### 4.2.5 Performance Verification

##### 4.2.5.1 Using SmartPerf-Host to Capture Trace

1. Configure according to [Using SmartPerf-Host to Analyze Application Performance](https://docs.openharmony.cn/pages/v5.1/zh-cn/application-dev/performance/performance-optimization-using-smartperf-host.md) documentation
2. Capture application startup or page jump Trace
3. View `parallelize build` related Trace segments

**Signs of Successful Parallelization**:
```
Child thread [Thread-XXXX] has "parallelize build" trace
```

##### 4.2.5.2 Key Performance Indicator Comparison

| Indicator | Measurement Method | Expected Effect |
|:----------|:-------------------|:----------------|
| **Page Response Latency** | Time from click event to first frame on screen | Reduce 10%~30% |
| **Main Thread Blocking Time** | Main thread execution creation task time | Significantly reduce |
| **First Frame Rendering Time** | First frame complete rendering time | Maintain or improve |

##### 4.2.5.3 Real Case Performance Data

According to official sample test data:

| Scenario | Parallel Creation Latency | Serial Creation Latency | Optimization Ratio |
|:---------|:-------------------------|:------------------------|:-------------------|
| **Declarative UI Page** | 87ms | 98.5ms | 13.2% |
| **BuilderNode Build** | 53.6ms | 74.8ms | 28.34% |
| **List/Grid List** | 26.2ms | 31.0ms | 18.3% |

#### 4.2.6 Refactoring Recommendations and Best Practices

##### 4.2.6.1 Refactoring Priority Recommendations

```
High Priority:
├── Complex pages with large number of components (component count > 100)
├── List/Grid long list scenarios (sub-item count > 50)
└── Page switching response latency > 100ms

Medium Priority:
├── Medium complexity pages (component count 50~100)
└── User feedback of obvious stuttering

Low Priority:
└── Simple interaction pages (component count < 50)
```

##### 4.2.6.2 Refactoring Step Recommendations

1. **Performance Analysis**: Use HiTrace or SmartPerf-Host to locate performance bottlenecks
2. **Scenario Identification**: Determine which pages or components are suitable for parallelization refactoring
3. **Small-scale Verification**: Select a single page for refactoring verification
4. **Effect Evaluation**: Compare performance indicators before and after refactoring
5. **Gradual Promotion**: Decide whether to promote to other pages based on verification results

##### 4.2.6.3 Common Issues

| Issue | Cause | Solution |
|:------|:------|:----------|
| Crash in Debug mode | Used external state variables in `ParallelizeUI` | Use `ParallelizeUI<T>` to pass via parameters |
| Performance improvement not obvious | Too few components, scheduling overhead greater than parallel benefit | Increase component count in parallel tasks, or cancel parallelization |
| Display content has delay | Parallel content displays in next frame | Change important content to serial creation |
| Compilation error | Used unsupported components | Remove unsupported components or adjust parallel area |

---

## ✅ 5. ArkTS Static Type Refactoring Best Practices

### 5.1 Efficiently Converting Large Batches of Dynamic Type Objects to Static Type Objects

**Scenario Description**

Original scenario, this module provides a universal method for object property traversal assignment, written in ts, input parameter `from` comes from other modules, `to` is maintained by this module, code as follows:
```typescript
function objectAssign(from: Object, to: Object): void {
  for (let key in from) {
    to[key] = from[key];
  }
}
```

Usually, for compatibility considerations, maintain interface behavior consistent with before refactoring. Refactoring scope is this module interface. From here, `to` becomes a static object, while `from` is a dynamic object used in static context. Restricted by ArkTS syntax, combined with static syntax's strong typing concept, need to clarify data structures in `from` and `to`, use interop interface implementation, refactored code as follows:
```typescript
'use static'

// Detailed data structure of `to` needs to be analyzed from business, and clarify types
// Here example `to` type only has one kind, has 3 properties, each property is optional
interface ToType {
  aNum?: number,
  bStr?: string,
  cBool?: boolean,
}

// Through property-by-property assignment, perform object data conversion, get property values from dynamic object `from`, need to use ESValue interface
objectAssign(from: ESValue, to: ToType):void {
  to.aNum = from.hasProperty('aNum') ? from.getProperty('aNum').toNumber() : 0;
  to.bStr = from.hasProperty('bStr') ? from.getProperty('bStr').toString() : '';
  to.cBool = from.hasProperty('cBool') ? from.getProperty('cBool').toBoolean() : false;
}
```

- First, in actual application business scenarios, there are two situations here

First, `ToType` is clearly multiple data structures, can be based on code with only one data structure type, extend `to` type, use union type declaration and conditional judgment logic to solve:
```typescript
'use static'

interface ToTypeA {
  aNum?: number,
  bStr?: string,
  cBool?: boolean,
}
interface ToTypeB {
  dNum?: number,
  eStr?: string
}
// `to` object is union type
type ToType = ToTypeA | ToTypeB;

// 1. Conditional branches for different processing logic of different types
// 2. Through property-by-property assignment, perform object data conversion, get property values from dynamic object `from`, need to use ESValue interface
function objectAssign(from: ESValue, to: ToType):void {
  if (to instanceof ToTypeA) {
    to = to as ToTypeA;
    to.aNum = from.hasProperty('aNum') ? from.getProperty('aNum').toNumber() : 0;
    to.bStr = from.hasProperty('bStr') ? from.getProperty('bStr').toString() : '';
    to.cBool = from.hasProperty('cBool') ? from.getProperty('cBool').toBoolean() : false;
  } else if (to instanceof ToTypeB) {
    to = to as ToTypeB;
    to.dNum = from.hasProperty('dNum') ? from.getProperty('dNum').toNumber() : 0;
    to.eStr = from.hasProperty('eStr') ? from.getProperty('eStr').toString() : '';
  }

}
```

Second, `ToType` cannot clarify specific structure. In this case, if want to maintain behavior consistent with before refactoring, in static can only use reflection method to implement. Currently reflection features are not clearly given, and in terms of performance, from industry general reflection technology solutions (such as Java's reflection mechanism), it is definitely not the optimal solution.
Therefore for this situation, to guarantee execution performance, combined with static syntax's strong typing concept, application business design angle needs optimization, modify "cannot clarify specific structure" design scheme to "can enumerate" design scheme, then use first situation to refactor.

- Second, based on the above scheme refactored, there are hidden instability hazards

If data structure is very complex, code lines will increase, leading to poor maintainability, and also because currently belongs to interop scenario, interop times will also increase, leading to intensified performance degradation. For such situation, simple migration refactoring scheme is no longer applicable, optimization direction is mainly to reduce interop times as much as possible.
From object conversion scenario perspective, analyze data provided by other modules and this module highly coupled, thus derive two additional refactoring schemes:
1. Evaluate including data providing module in static refactoring scope, then execution object conversion context is pure static:
```typescript
'use static'

interface FromType {
  aNum?: number,
  bStr?: string,
  cBool?: boolean,
}
interface ToType {
  aNum?: number,
  bStr?: string,
}

// Direct value assignment, performance-sensitive scenario optimal solution
function objectAssign(from: FromType, to: ToType):void {
  to.aNum = from.aNum;
  to.bStr = from.bStr;
}
```

2. Data providing module cannot be refactored, serialize transmission data, for example JSON processing, pass as json string parameter, greatly reduce interop value fetching operations
```typescript
'use static'

interface FromType {
  aNum?: number,
  bStr?: string,
  cBool?: boolean,
}
class ToType {
  aNum?: number,
  bStr?: string,
}

// Through JSON parsing to static object, subsequent direct value assignment
function objectAssign(from: string, to: ToType):void {
  const fromData = JSON.parse<FromType>(from, Class.from<FromType>());
  to.aNum = fromData.aNum;
  to.bStr = fromData.bStr;
}
```

### 5.2 ESValue Basic Usage
[ESValue Basic Usage](https://gitcode.com/openharmony/arkcompiler_runtime_core/blob/master/static_core/plugins/ets/runtime/interop_js/docs/ESValueApi_cn.md)

### 5.3 How to Use Dynamic Enum Passed into Static
```TS
  // ========== Dynamic file DynamicModule.ets ==========
  // Enum defined in dynamic code
  export enum ProcessStatus {
    IDLE = 0,
    RUNNING = 1
  }


  //  ========== Static file StaticModule.ets ==========
  'use static'

  /**
   * Handle enum value passed from dynamic code
   * @param statusValue Dynamic enum value (Any type)
   */
  function handleProcessStatus(statusValue: Any): void {
    // 1. Load dynamic module
    let dynamicModule: ESValue = ESValue.load("library/src/main/ets/DynamicModule");

    // 2. Get enum object
    let statusEnum: ESValue = dynamicModule.getProperty("ProcessStatus");

    // 3. Get each enum value and convert to number
    let idleStatus: number = statusEnum.getProperty("IDLE").toNumber();
    let runningStatus: number = statusEnum.getProperty("RUNNING").toNumber();

    // 4. Use switch for branch processing
    switch (statusValue) {
      case idleStatus:
        console.log("Current status: Idle");
        break;
      case runningStatus:
        console.log("Current status: Running");
        break;
      default:
        console.log("Unknown status");
        break;
    }
  }

  Dynamic using static enum, normal import usage.

```

### 5.4 How to Use Dynamic Record Passed into Static
  In dynamic language, Record is a type, in static language it's a class. When passing across languages, runtime actually gets object, not Record class
```TS
// ========== Dynamic file DynamicModule.ets ==========
let dataRecord:Record<string,string> = {
  "name":"jack",
  "age":"20"
}
transferToSta(dataRecord);


//  ========== Static file StaticModule.ets ==========
'use static'

/**
  * Handle record passed from dynamic code
  * @param dataRecord Dynamic record (Any type)
  */
transferToSta(dataRecord:Any):void{
  let dataRecordDyna:ESValue = ESValue.wrap(dataRecord);
  //1: Get value corresponding to specified key
  let valueDyna:string = dataRecordDyna.getProperty("name").toString();
  //2: Traverse dynamic record in static
  for(const d of dataRecordDyna.keys()){
    //3: Print dynamic record key value
    console.log("dataRecordDyna keys:"+d.toString());
    //4: Print value in dynamic record
    console.log("dataRecordDyna value:"+dataRecordDyna.getProperty(d.toString()).toString());
  }
}
```

### 5.5 How to Use Static Record Passed into Dynamic
```typescript
  	  // ========== Static file StaticModule.ets ==========
  	  'use static'

  	  /**
  	   * Return Record from static code
  	   * @returns Record<string, string> Return key-value record
  	   */
  	  export function getRecordFromSta(): Record<string, string> {
  	    // Create static Record object
  	    let dataRecord: Record<string, string> = {
  	      "name": "jack",
  	      "age": "20"
  	    }
  	    return dataRecord;  // Return for dynamic code use
  	  }

  	  /**
  	   * Traverse Record in static code, pass each value to dynamic code for processing
  	   */
  	  export function processRecords(): void {
  	    // Get static Record
  	    let data: Record<string, string> = getRecordFromSta();

  	    // Can normally traverse Record in static
  	    for (let k of data.keys()) {
  	      let v: string = data[k];  // Get value corresponding to key

  	      // Call dynamic code method via ESValue, pass wrapped value
  	      ESValue.load("DynamicModule").invokeMethod("processRecordValue", ESValue.wrap(v));
  	    }
  	  }

  	  // ========== Dynamic file DynamicModule.ets ==========
  	  /**
  	   * Handle record returned from static code
  	   */
  	  function processRecord(): void {
  	    // Call static code to get Record
  	    let recordSta:Record<string, string> = getRecordFromSta();

  	    // ✓ Direct access via index - unaware usage, can normally get value
  	    let value:string = recordSta["name"];

  	    // ✗ Cannot traverse static record in dynamic, following is wrong writing
  	    // Object.entries(recordSta).forEach((key, value) => {
  	    //   console.log(key, value);
  	    // });
      processRecordValue(value);
  	  }

  	  /**
  	   * Handle single record value passed from static code
  	   * @param value A value in Record
  	   */
  	  export function processRecordValue(value: string): void {
  	    console.log("processRecordValue:" + value);
  	  }

```

### 5.6 JSON Serialization and Deserialization Operations, Dynamic-Static Comparison

- Dynamic syntax, JSON serialization and deserialization as follows:
```typescript
const str = "{ \"a\": 1, \"b\": \{ \"c\": true \} }";

// Dynamic deserialization
const jsonObj = JSON.parse(str);
// Dynamic serialization
const jsonStr = JSON.stringify(jsonObj);
```

- In static environment, need to distinguish processing methods according to different application scenarios

**Scenario 1: JSON string expressed content has business data with clear data structure**

Use `JSON.parse` and `JSON.stringify` for type-safe serialization and deserialization operations.

```typescript
'use static'

let loginInfoStr:string = "{ \"userId\": \"123\", \"userInfo\": \{ \"token\": \"xxxxx\", \"vip\": true \} }";

class userInfoCls {
    token?: string;
    vip?: boolean;
}

class loginInfoCls {
    userId?: string;
    userInfo?: userInfoCls;
}

// Static deserialization (basic usage)
try {
    const jsonObj: = JSON.parse<loginInfoCls>(loginInfoStr, Class.from<loginInfoCls>());
    // Static serialization
    const jsonStr:string = JSON.stringify(jsonObj);
} catch (error) {
    const err: Error = error as Error;
    console.error(`JSON parsing failed: ${err.message}`);
}

// Static deserialization (with reviver and options)
// reviver: Used for data conversion, filtering, validation and other custom processing
const reviver = (key: string, value: Any): Any => {
    if (key == "userId" && typeof value === 'String') {
        return `id_${(value as string)}`;  // Convert userId
    }
    return value;
};

// options: Used to control parsing behavior, mainly BigInt processing strategy
const options: jsonx.ParseOptions = {
    bigIntMode: jsonx.BigIntMode.PARSE_AS_BIGINT
} as jsonx.ParseOptions;

try {
    const jsonObj = JSON.parse<loginInfoCls>(loginInfoStr, reviver, Class.from<loginInfoCls>(), options);
} catch (error) {
    const err: Error = error as Error;
    console.error(`JSON parsing failed: ${err.message}`);
}
```

**Scenario 2: JSON string content unclear, and need to perform add/delete/modify/query operations on JSON result**

Use `JSON.parseJsonElement` and `JSON.stringifyJsonElement` for flexible JSON operations.

```typescript
'use static'

const str:string = "{ \"a\": 1, \"b\": \{ \"c\": true \} }";

// Static deserialization (basic usage)
try {
    const jsonElement: jsonx.JsonElement = JSON.parseJsonElement(str);

    // Get value - strict mode (key not exists will throw exception)
    const aVal: jsonx.JsonElement = jsonElement.getElement("a");
    console.info(aVal.asInteger());  // 1

    // Get value - loose mode (key not exists returns undefined)
    const notExistVal = jsonElement.tryGetElement("notExist");
    if (notExistVal !== undefined) {
        console.info(notExistVal.asString());
    }

    // Set value
    jsonElement.setElement('d', jsonx.JsonElement.createString('dVal'));

    // Delete element
    const removed = jsonElement.removeElement('a');

    // Static serialization
    const jsonStr:string = JSON.stringifyJsonElement(jsonElement);
} catch (error) {
    const err: Error = error as Error;
    console.error(`JSON operation failed: ${err.message}`);
}

// Static deserialization (with reviver)
// reviver: Used for data conversion, filtering, validation and other custom processing
const reviver = (key: string, value: jsonx.JsonElement): jsonx.JsonElement => {
    if (key === "a") {
        return jsonx.JsonElement.createInteger(value.asInteger() * 2);  // Convert a value
    }
    return value;
};

try {
    const jsonElement: jsonx.JsonElement = JSON.parseJsonElement(str, reviver);
} catch (error) {
    const err: Error = error as Error;
    console.error(`JSON parsing failed: ${err.message}`);
}
```

**Notes**

1. **Error Handling**: JSON operations in static syntax may throw exceptions, must wrap with try-catch
2. **Strict Mode vs Loose Mode**:
   - Strict mode: `getElement`/`getString`/`getInteger`, etc., key not exists will throw exception
   - Loose mode: `tryGetElement`/`tryGetString`/`tryGetInteger`, etc., key not exists returns undefined or default value
3. **BigInt Handling**: Control big integer parsing behavior via `ParseOptions`'s `bigIntMode`:
   - `DEFAULT`: Does not support BigInt, exceeding long range will error
   - `PARSE_AS_BIGINT`: When integer less than -(2^53-1) or greater than (2^53-1), parse as BigInt
   - `ALWAYS_PARSE_AS_BIGINT`: All integers parse as BigInt
4. **Performance Consideration**: When data structure clear, using `JSON.parse` has better performance, when structure unclear, use `JSON.parseJsonElement`

**Related Materials**
[JSON](https://gitcode.com/openharmony/docs/blob/OpenHarmony_feature_20250702/zh-cn/application-dev/reference/native-lib/arkts-sta-json.md)
[jsonx](https://gitcode.com/openharmony/docs/blob/OpenHarmony_feature_20250702/zh-cn/application-dev/reference/native-lib/arkts-sta-jsonx.md)


### 5.7 EAWorker Usage and Submitting Tasks to Main Thread for Execution
[EAWorker Basic Usage](https://gitcode.com/openharmony/docs/blob/OpenHarmony_feature_20250328/zh-cn/application-dev/reference/native-lib/eaworker_managed.md)

EAWorker behind is an independently running thread, needs developer to manually manage lifecycle.
Following is example code:
```typescript
// ========== Static file StaticModule.ets ==========
'use static'

export function foo(){
//Second parameter indicates need to enable interop, if submitted tasks contain dynamic code, will start a dynamic virtual machine
let eaw:EAWorker = new EAWorker("workerTask",true);
//Start EAWorker
eaw.start();
//Set priority
eaw.setPrioity(WorkerPriority.PRIORITY_HIGH);
//Submit task to worker
eaw.run(taskA)
//Actively destroy Worker thread resources
eaw.join();
}
//Task executed in child thread
export function taskA():void{
   //If some tasks must execute in main thread, can use PostToMain interface
   const job:EAWorker.Job = EAWorker.postToMain<boolean>(taskB);
   //Get task execution result
   let jobresult:boolean = job.Await();
}

export function taskB():boolean{
   /// Tasks that must execute in main thread
   return true;
}
```

### 5.8 transfer, System Object Dynamic-Static Conversion

transfer is ArkTS-Sta exclusive system object conversion tool, used for interop conversion of system objects between ArkTS-Dyn and ArkTS-Sta.

**Scenario 1: ArkTS-Dyn Object Converted to ArkTS-Sta Object**

When need to pass system object in ArkTS-Dyn environment to ArkTS-Sta environment for use, use `transfer.transferStatic` for conversion:

```typescript
'use static'

import { transfer } from '@kit.ArkTS'

// Function in ArkTS-Sta environment, receives ArrayBuffer object from ArkTS-Dyn
function processDynamicArrayBuffer(dynamicArrayBuffer: Any): void {
  // Convert ArkTS-Dyn's ArrayBuffer to ArkTS-Sta's ArrayBuffer
  let staticArrayBuffer: ArrayBuffer = transfer.transferStatic(dynamicArrayBuffer, 'InteropTransferTransferHelper') as ArrayBuffer;

  // Now can use converted ArrayBuffer in ArkTS-Sta environment
  console.info(`ArrayBuffer length: ${staticArrayBuffer.byteLength}`);

  // Create Int8Array view for operation
  let int8Array = new Int8Array(staticArrayBuffer);
  for (let i: int = 0; i < int8Array.length; i++) {
    console.info(`index ${i}: ${int8Array[i]}`);
  }
}
```

**Scenario 2: ArkTS-Sta Object Converted to ArkTS-Dyn Object**

When need to pass system object in ArkTS-Sta environment to ArkTS-Dyn environment for use, use `transfer.transferDynamic` for conversion:

```typescript
'use static'

import { transfer } from '@kit.ArkTS'
import { ESValue } from '@kit.ArkTS'

// Create ArrayBuffer in ArkTS-Sta environment
let staticArrayBuffer: ArrayBuffer = new ArrayBuffer(8);
let view = new Int8Array(staticArrayBuffer);
view[0] = 42;

// Convert ArkTS-Sta's ArrayBuffer to ArkTS-Dyn's ArrayBuffer
let dynamicArrayBuffer: Any = transfer.transferDynamic(staticArrayBuffer, 'InteropTransferHelper');

// Call function in ArkTS-Dyn environment
let jsModule:ESValue = ESValue.load('./dynamic-module');
let processFunc:ESValue = jsModule.getProperty('processArrayBuffer');
processFunc.invoke(ESValue.wrap(dynamicArrayBuffer));
```

**Scenario 3: Cross-thread System Object Passing**

In multi-thread scenarios, when need to pass system objects between different threads, also need to use transfer for conversion:

```typescript
'use static'

import { transfer, taskpool } from '@kit.ArkTS'
import { ESValue } from '@kit.ArkTS'

// Create ArrayBuffer in main thread
let mainArrayBuffer: ArrayBuffer = new ArrayBuffer(1024);

// Convert main thread's ArrayBuffer to cross-thread transferable format
let transferableArrayBuffer: Any = transfer.transferDynamic(mainArrayBuffer, 'InteropTransferHelper');

// Function in child thread
function workerTask(data: Any): void {
  // Convert data back to ArkTS-Sta's ArrayBuffer in child thread
  let workerArrayBuffer: ArrayBuffer = transfer.transferStatic(data, 'InteropTransferHelper') as ArrayBuffer;

  console.info(`Worker received ArrayBuffer length: ${workerArrayBuffer.byteLength}`);
}

// Create task and execute
let task:Task = new taskpool.Task(workerTask, transferableArrayBuffer);
taskpool.execute(task).then(() => {
  console.info('Task completed');
});
```

**Scenario 4: Handling UI Event Objects**

When need to pass UI event object in ArkTS-Dyn to ArkTS-Sta for processing:

```typescript
'use static'

import { transfer } from '@kit.ArkTS'
import { ESValue } from '@kit.ArkTS'

// Handle ClickEvent from ArkTS-Dyn in ArkTS-Sta environment
function handleDynamicClickEvent(dynamicClickEvent: Any): void {
  // Convert ArkTS-Dyn's ClickEvent to ArkTS-Sta's ClickEvent
  let staticClickEvent = transfer.transferStatic(dynamicClickEvent, 'ArkUI.ClickEvent');

  // Use converted ClickEvent
  console.info(`Click event received`);
}
```

**Supported Conversion Object Types**

transfer supports conversion of the following system objects:

| key Value | System Object Type |
|-----------|-------------------|
| "InteropTransferHelper" | ArrayBuffer |
| "ArkUI.NavDestinationInfo" | NavDestinationInfo |
| "ArkUI.NavigationInfo" | NavigationInfo |
| "ArkUI.RouterPageInfo" | RouterPageInfo |
| "AbilityKit.UIAbilityContext" | UIAbilityContext |
| "ArkUI.DrawableDescriptor" | DrawableDescriptor |
| "ArkUI.DragEvent" | DragEvent |
| "ArkUI.KeyEvent" | KeyEvent |
| "ArkUI.TouchEvent" | TouchEvent |
| "ArkUI.MouseEvent" | MouseEvent |
| "ArkUI.AxisEvent" | AxisEvent |
| "ClickEvent" | ClickEvent |
| "ArkUI.HoverEvent" | HoverEvent |
| "ArkUI.EventTargetInfo" | EventTargetInfo |
| "ArkUI.TouchTestInfo" | TouchTestInfo |
| "ArkUI.ScrollableTargetInfo" | ScrollableTargetInfo |
| "ArkUI.FrameNode" | FrameNode |
| "ArkUI.RenderNode" | RenderNode |
| "ArkUI.UIContext" | UIContext |
| "ImageKit.PixelMap" | PixelMap |
| "ImageKit.ImageSource" | ImageSource |
| "ImageKit.Picture" | Picture |
| "ImageKit.ImageCreator" | ImageCreator |
| "ImageKit.ImagePacker" | ImagePacker |
| "ImageKit.ImageReceiver" | ImageReceiver |
| "ArkWeb.WebMessagePort" | WebMessagePort |
| "ArkWeb.JsResult" | JsResult |

**Notes**

1. **ArkTS-Sta Only**: transfer can only be used in ArkTS-Sta environment, ArkTS-Dyn environment cannot use
2. **Key Value Must Match**: Must use correct key value during conversion, otherwise will throw error "Transfer Error. The input name is not supported!"
3. **Error Handling**: Recommend wrapping conversion operations with try-catch to handle possible conversion errors
4. **Performance Consideration**: System object conversion has certain overhead, should avoid frequent conversion
5. **Object Lifecycle**: Converted object shares underlying resources with original object, need to pay attention to object lifecycle management

**Error Handling Example**

```typescript
'use static'

import { transfer } from '@kit.ArkTS'

function safeTransfer(dynamicObj: Any): Object | undefined {
  try {
    return transfer.transferStatic(dynamicObj, 'InteropTransferHelper');
  } catch (error) {
    const err: Error = error as Error;
    console.error(`Transfer failed: ${err.message}`);
    return undefined;
  }
}
```

**Related Materials**
[@ohos.transfer (System Object Conversion Tool)](https://gitcode.com/openharmony/docs/blob/OpenHarmony_feature_20250702/zhzh-cn/application-dev/reference/apis-arkts/js-apis-transfer.md)

### 5.9 napi2ani Refactoring, Business Object Deep Coupling

#### 5.9.1 Background Knowledge

Official materials already provide napi2ani migration guide, however, for complex type definitions, only very simple refactoring examples are provided. This case combines actual application scenarios to provide a refactoring method, solving how to perform ANI migration when application business deeply couples NAPI. This case will also use Taihe to reduce migration workload.

#### 5.9.2 Status Review

First show case's module architecture design based on NAPI:

<img src="data:image/png;base64," width="70%">
